stories.title: Pentagon used Elon Musk's Grok AI to fire 2k missiles at Iran, official says
stories.paragraph: A US official has claimed that the Pentagon used xAI's Grok model in targeting decisions that resulted in the firing of approximately 2,000 missiles at Iran. The claim raises significant questions about the role of commercial AI systems in military operations and the oversight mechanisms governing their use. If accurate, it would represent one of the most consequential documented deployments of a commercially available large language model in active combat.

stories.title: Bernie Sanders unveils $7T plan to give Americans control of AI industry
stories.paragraph: Senator Bernie Sanders has proposed a $7 trillion plan that would shift significant control of the AI industry toward public ownership or oversight, positioning it as a counterweight to corporate dominance of the technology. The proposal enters a crowded legislative environment where AI regulation is actively contested at the federal level. The $7 trillion figure is roughly comparable to the combined market capitalization of the largest US technology firms and signals the scale Sanders envisions for public investment or acquisition.

stories.title: Norway imposes near ban on AI in elementary school
stories.paragraph: Norway's government has introduced restrictions that amount to a near-total ban on AI tool use in elementary school settings, citing concerns about the impact on foundational skill development in young children. The policy makes Norway one of the first European countries to implement age-specific AI restrictions at the national level within public education. The move follows broader debate in many countries about when and how students should be permitted to use generative AI for schoolwork.

stories.title: A new bill takes aim at government pressure to silence lawful online speech
stories.paragraph: The Electronic Frontier Foundation has highlighted new legislation designed to limit the ability of government agencies to pressure platforms into removing or suppressing lawful speech online. While not exclusively focused on AI, the bill has direct implications for AI-generated content moderation and the role of government in shaping what AI systems surface or filter. The EFF frames the bill as a response to documented cases of federal agencies informally coercing platforms to act against constitutionally protected expression.

theme: AI Costs, Business Models & Industry Tensions

stories.title: Companies rein in AI usage as costs strain budgets
stories.paragraph: The Financial Times reports that a growing number of companies are scaling back AI adoption after finding that the costs of running large language model-based tools are exceeding anticipated budgets. Some organizations are implementing usage caps or switching to cheaper, smaller models to manage expenditure. The trend suggests that the initial wave of enterprise AI enthusiasm is giving way to more disciplined cost-benefit analysis.

stories.title: Anthropic "pauses" token-based billing for its Claude Agent SDK
stories.paragraph: Anthropic has temporarily suspended token-based billing for its Claude Agent SDK, according to Ars Technica, following what appears to be pushback or complications related to the pricing model for agentic workloads. Token-based billing for agent tasks is particularly contentious because multi-step agent loops can consume tokens at rates that are difficult for developers to predict or cap. The pause signals that Anthropic is still working to find a billing structure that suits the distinct usage patterns of autonomous agent workflows.

stories.title: Generative AI Is Having Its Herbalife Moment
stories.paragraph: A piece in What We Lost draws a comparison between the current state of generative AI commercialization and Herbalife's multi-level marketing model, arguing that a significant portion of AI-related revenue is being generated by selling AI tools to people who then try to resell AI services, rather than through genuine end-user productivity gains. The author contends that this creates a self-reinforcing hype cycle that obscures weak underlying demand from non-AI-industry consumers. The analogy is pointed at the proliferation of AI consultants, courses, and reseller businesses that have emerged around tools like ChatGPT and Claude.

stories.title: Amazon employees say they're facing termination for backing data center limits
stories.paragraph: Employees at Amazon have told The Verge they are facing disciplinary action, including potential termination, after publicly supporting a proposed moratorium on new data center construction in the Seattle area. The workers are associated with Amazon Employees for Climate Justice (AECJ), a group that has previously organized around the company's environmental commitments. The situation highlights growing internal friction at major tech companies over the energy and land-use footprint of AI infrastructure.

stories.title: Aikido Code Audit
stories.paragraph: Security firm Aikido has launched a Code Audit product that uses AI to identify complex vulnerabilities in codebases, targeting issues that traditional static analysis tools tend to miss, such as multi-step logic flaws and context-dependent security bugs. The tool is positioned as a complement to existing scanners rather than a replacement, focusing on vulnerability classes that require understanding code flow across files. Aikido's announcement comes as AI-assisted code review is becoming a more crowded space, with competitors including GitHub's Copilot Autofix and Snyk's AI features.

stories.title: Hide Secrets from AI Agents and NPM install using Airgap
stories.paragraph: A technical post by Sven Sauleau describes an approach called "airgapping" to prevent AI coding agents and package managers like NPM from inadvertently accessing or exfiltrating secrets stored in a development environment. The method involves isolating credential stores and environment variables from processes that invoke AI tools or run dependency installs. As AI agents gain broader file system and network access in developer workflows, the post argues that treating them with the same skepticism as third-party processes is a necessary security posture.

stories.title: It Is Trivially Easy to Use Reddit to Manipulate AI Search
stories.paragraph: Research highlighted by 404 Media demonstrates that AI-powered search tools can be manipulated by seeding Reddit with targeted content, which the systems then surface as authoritative information. Because many AI search products weight Reddit highly due to its inclusion in training data and its prominence in web indexes, fabricated or promotional posts can directly influence AI-generated answers. The researchers found the process required minimal technical sophistication, raising concerns about the reliability of AI search for consequential queries.

stories.title: Quoting Sean Lynch
stories.paragraph: Simon Willison has highlighted a comment by Sean Lynch on Hacker News that reframes the primary value proposition of the Model Context Protocol (MCP). Lynch argues that MCP's most meaningful contribution is not tool invocation but rather keeping authentication flows outside of the agent's context window, and potentially outside the agent harness entirely, which reduces security exposure and token consumption. Lynch goes further to suggest that an idealized version of MCP might function purely as an auth gateway, with everything else being secondary.

stories.title: MiniMax M3 vs. GLM 5.2: Codegen comparison across autonomous coding tasks
stories.paragraph: A comparative evaluation published by ThinkWright pits MiniMax's M3 model against Zhipu AI's GLM 5.2 across a range of autonomous coding tasks, including code generation, debugging, and multi-file editing. The analysis provides concrete pass-rate metrics for each model across task categories, offering practitioners a data point for choosing between two Chinese-developed open or semi-open models for coding use cases. Both models have been positioned as cost-efficient alternatives to frontier models from OpenAI and Anthropic for code-heavy workloads.

stories.title: Linux Maintainer Greg Kroah-Hartman Says AI Tools Now Useful, Finding Real Bugs
stories.paragraph: Linux kernel maintainer Greg Kroah-Hartman has stated that AI-generated bug reports submitted to the Linux project have improved to the point where they are now identifying genuine issues rather than producing the low-quality, hallucinated reports that prompted past frustration from kernel developers. Kroah-Hartman previously warned contributors about submitting AI-generated noise to the kernel mailing list, making his revised assessment notable. He did not name specific tools but indicated that the signal-to-noise ratio has shifted meaningfully in recent months.

stories.title: Is AI ruining our skills? Early results are in – and they're not good
stories.paragraph: A Nature news article surveys early empirical research on whether reliance on AI tools is degrading human cognitive and professional skills, finding that initial results across several studies suggest measurable negative effects on tasks ranging from writing to clinical reasoning. One cited study found that participants who used AI assistance for writing produced work rated higher in quality but showed reduced ability to perform the same tasks unaided afterward. Researchers note that the evidence base is still limited and that distinguishing skill atrophy from task offloading remains methodologically difficult.

stories.title: AI Engineer Claims to Have Cracked Linear A
stories.paragraph: An AI engineer has publicly claimed to have deciphered Linear A, the undeciphered writing system of the ancient Minoan civilization, using large language models and pattern-matching techniques applied to the script's known corpus. Linear A has resisted professional decipherment for over a century, and mainstream archaeologists and linguists remain deeply skeptical of AI-assisted claims in this area. The blog post presents the methodology but has not undergone peer review, and no academic institutions have publicly validated the results.

stories.title: I solved my mystery fatigue with AI
stories.paragraph: A personal account published on Substack describes how an individual used AI tools, likely a large language model, to help diagnose a persistent and unresolved fatigue condition after conventional medical consultations failed to identify a cause. The author details using the AI to synthesize symptoms, research potential conditions, and generate questions for physicians, ultimately leading to a diagnosis. The piece is representative of a growing genre of first-person narratives about AI-assisted self-diagnosis, which raises both interest in AI's utility for healthcare navigation and questions about appropriate reliance on unvalidated medical guidance.
